defs: To make a tender of; to offer or tender.

records.ety: [Aphetic form of attend. See Attend, Tend to move, and cf. Tender one that tends or attends.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Tended; p. pr. & vb. n. Tending.]

defs: To accompany as an assistant or protector; to care for the wants of; to look after; to watch; to guard; as, shepherds tend their flocks.
quotes: And flaming ministers to watch and tend Their earthly charge.
There 's not a sparrow or a wren, There 's not a blade of autumn grain, Which the four seasons do not tend And tides of life and increase lend.

defs: To be attentive to; to note carefully; to attend to.
quotes: Being to descend A ladder much in height, I did not tend My way well down.

defs: To wait, as attendants or servants; to serve; to attend; -- with on or upon.
quotes: Was he not companion with the riotous knights That tend upon my father?

records.ety: [F. tendre, L. tendere, tensum and tentum, to stretch, extend, direct one's course, tend; akin to Gr. <?/ to stretch, Skr. tan. See Thin, and cf. Tend to attend, Contend, Intense, Ostensible, Portent, Tempt, Tender to offer, Tense, a.]

defs: To move in a certain direction; -- usually with to or towards.
quotes: Two gentlemen tending towards that sight.
Thus will this latter, as the former world, Still tend from bad to worse.
The clouds above me to the white Alps tend.

defs: To be directed, as to any end, object, or purpose; to aim; to have or give a leaning; to exert activity or influence; to serve as a means; to contribute; as, our petitions, if granted, might tend to our destruction.
quotes: The thoughts of the diligent tend only to plenteousness; but of every one that is hasty only to want.
The laws of our religion tend to the universal happiness of mankind.
